topic: Unit 3: Studying Spoken and Written Language
subject: english-language

items.q: What is a speech act according to Searle/Austin?
items.correct: An utterance that performs an action — promising, ordering, apologising, declaring. Speech acts have locutionary (what's said), illocutionary (intended force) and perlocutionary (effect) dimensions.
items.misconception: A speech act is any time someone speaks.
items.random_wrong: A speech act is a performance or a play.
items.why: Speech act theory analyses how language DOES things, not just describes things. 'I now pronounce you married' is a declaration, not a description. Students miss that utterances can be acts in themselves.

items.q: What is the difference between inflectional and derivational morphology?
items.correct: Inflectional morphemes mark grammatical features without changing word class (cat/cats, walk/walked); derivational morphemes create new words, often changing word class (happy → happiness, teach → teacher).
items.misconception: Inflection and derivation are two words for the same process.
items.random_wrong: Inflectional morphology applies only to verbs and derivational only to nouns.
items.why: The difference matters for word formation rules. English has very limited inflection but rich derivation. Students conflate the two, missing how morphology builds vocabulary vs marks grammar.

items.q: What is the difference between transitive and intransitive verbs?
items.correct: Transitive verbs take a direct object ('she ate the cake'); intransitive verbs don't ('she slept'). Some verbs are both depending on use ('she ran' / 'she ran the company').
items.misconception: Transitive verbs are action verbs and intransitive are linking verbs.
items.random_wrong: Transitive verbs end in -ed and intransitive in -ing.
items.why: Transitivity is about object-taking, not action vs state. Both transitive and intransitive verbs can be action verbs. Students conflate transitivity with verb category, missing the syntactic criterion.
Correct answer: Received Pronunciation. “Regional Pronunciation” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Vocabulary and grammar. “Pronunciation only” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Roughly what % of UK speak with traditional RP?
items.why: The real figure is 3 %. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
